speaker: interviewer
text: --5. Survivor is Gerda Marcus Wilchfort. And the day is December 17, 1995. And Gerda, we left off on the last tape with you discussing that you got married. And the war was over. What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed a few more months in, in Bern. And my mother was in Bern, too. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we started to learn the fate of the other Jews, you know, that had not been as fortunate as we have, including my father, and my grandparents, and everybody that we had left behind. And we had no idea up to this point, till the newspapers came out after the war, and the camps were liberated. And it was a very sad time for us. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a while, the, the Red Cross approached us, you know, because they didn't want the refugees to stay there. A few stayed behind. But-- and they put us back on the train. We packed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community gave me a sewing machine because, as they said, what can we do for you to facilitate a new-- a new beginning, to the-- I said well, if you want to do something for me, give me enough money to buy a sewing machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought a sewing machine, a treadle machine. And we took it along. And my new husband and my mother, we went back to Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my uncle was deported also. But my-- his wife was not Jewish. And she was there. And we stayed with her for awhile until we found a place to stay. There, there was, of course, a housing shortage in Brussels. And with the sewing machine, we started pretty soon to, to do sewing. And everything was needed in Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we found a place we could buy fabric, five yards at a time. So each of us went to the store and bought the same fabric. And we started to make blouses like I made in the blouse factory in, in Bern.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had learned how to make patterns. And as we-- we made blouses. And my mother started to sell them. And later on, that became a real nice, little business. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother had remarried. Well, she wasn't married at first. But then when we had noticed that my father was killed, and the, the, the Red Cross confirmed that he was nowhere to be found, and-- so she had papers. And she got remarried to Max [? Stern. ?] And he was part of the business. And he cut the blouses. And I sewed the blouses. And we had home workers who, who did, you know, more of the work. And it was a nice, little business. My mother had travelled with it. And-- but then my son was born, Gary. And my mother and my husband went to the city hall and wanted to register him. So they said he is an Allemand non-enemy, a non-enemy German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had enough of German. And my mother said, this kid has never seen Germany. What does he-- why is he a German? His parents are German. He's German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that prompted us to try to go-- to come to America. We said, we're not going to be here, again, German, because the Germans were very hated, of course. You know, they lost the war. But it was-- later that changed. And if we had waited it out, we might have had an easier life in Brussels because we were already established and everything. But we said, no way, we don't want to be Germans again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my stepfather already had a brother in Vineland, New Jersey, in America. And we had already made a little bit of money. And we were there two or three years and saved the money because we knew that was-- we have to go where-- as a matter of fact, we tried to go to Ecuador and-- where my husband had an uncle and aunt. And that didn't pan out. It was always this question of, where do we go? And in the middle of everything, I had a baby. And my mother said, what do you-- you don't know where you're going to go and what's going to be, and you want a baby? I said, yes, I want a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: My stepfather said, alrighty then. He said, when we eat, four people will eat. The baby will eat. It's all right. And so finally, we got the papers for America. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gary was born in '47. And I think, in '48, we, all five, went on a French boat. We went to Le Havre. And we came to America. And my stepfather's brother had bought a farm already across the street from them for my parents. And we stayed long enough to help them paint and furnish it and get it started. And-- but we didn't want to stay in the country. And we didn't have any-- not enough money to buy a farm. And I didn't like that life on the farm. And we went to Philadelphia. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my husband found, first, a very low-paying job. And then-- and he had to learn how to drive. He didn't even know how to drive. We had no means. You know, we had lost all these years, all these teenage years. We had no skills, we had-- other than my little sewing. But it wasn't enough. And so they told him-- he learned how to drive in a very short time, of course. And we bought a little Jeep truck. And my parents were on the farm with the eggs. So he got eggs. And he started to sell eggs. And he built up a nice, little business over the years. We didn't get rich, but we made a living. And the Jewish community in Philadelphia sent him from one to the other. And, and we lived there for 12, 15 years. And my second son, Peter, was born. But then things started to get-- go bad. And the people moved in all directions. And neighborhoods changed. And so we decided to make a change. And we came to Tucson, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an occupation in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was always a seamstress, more or less, you know. But-- I-- we-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the fate of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I learned quite recently that my grandparents on my mother's side were sent to Theresienstadt. That I knew-- but that my grandmother died very shortly after arriving in Theresienstadt and that my grandfather was sent to Auschwitz about a year later and never came back. My other grandmother was always prone to suicide. She tried a few times. But she was never successful. I think she never did-- wanted to be successful. But when it came to the end, she did commit suicide. And she sat in the kitchen and opened-- had her hair done-- she was a real lady. And she sat in her kitchen and opened the gas. And she died, which was really for the best. And I heard since that she wasn't the only one, that a lot, a lot of people did this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was deported from Drancy. And I never heard from him again. And I heard-- from one person, we heard, after the war, that he was in a-- in one of those cattle cars. And he must have died right there because he was in a bad condition. I don't think he ever arrived in any camp. And I hope he didn't because the quicker they died, the less they suffered. And so, my uncle, we never heard again from my uncle, either. He was deported from Drancy. And my in-laws, Werner's parents were in Theresienstadt and must have died there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened to Werner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Werner was fine. We came to-- we came together to America. We had the two children, Gary and Peter. And we came to Tucson. And we had a business here together, which was really based on sewing, on my sewing. But he worked with me. And unfortunately, he developed leukemia. And he died just a day after his 51st birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we end, I'd just like to ask you few questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, mainly thanks to my mother. She was-- it, it takes a very strong person to survive and to do-- make the right decisions-- and luck. You know, we could have been caught in France on the way to Switzerland. That was just a split second of, of life or death. We could have-- we had another-- as a matter of fact, this couple who got us into contact with this Underground, they did not make it. They, they looked very Jewish. And they had an Eastern European accent. And they were caught. And we had the communication-- their children were separated from them. They had two children, too. And they were sent to Auschwitz. And we never heard from them. I mean, not everybody made it to Switzerland. That was a touch-and-go situation, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, is she still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but she made it to almost 92. She is-- she was a survivor. She was not a very healthy person. But she survived till she was almost 92, just a few days shy of 92.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the family, the, the [NON-ENGLISH] the, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]? No, they all made it. They, they were with us in Bern. I don't think they were with us on the-- on, on this-- in this camp in Weinberg. But they-- her husband was already in America. And her oldest son came to America during the war. And no, they're all-- they-- well, of course, Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] was older than my mother. She must be 100, now. And she may be alive or not. I don't know. I, I tried to-- last time I heard, she was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have contact anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not with her, but with her daughter. And I told her daughter that my mother had died. But she never responded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams at all about what you experienced during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I have dreams about my husband dying. I-- but not about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what advice would you like to give your children? You-- I know you have grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice would you like to give your children and your grandchildren and future generations after having survived what you did? What advice would you like to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, of course, to, to make decisions-- not to be led and not to go with the flow. Make your own decisions according to the situation that you're in. And keep control over your life as much as possible. And also never to give up because we were in-- sometimes in Belgium, before the deportations and-- started, and when the war went so well for the Germans, and we were quite desperate sometimes. And we were talking about suicide and all this. But we always said well, we can always do that. You know, there's always a way out like that. But you have to keep on trying and going on and, and hope for the best. Things can change and-- in a minute, you know, in a day. And never to give up, and persevere, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother and her parents and her brother, Gerhard.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your mother is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, it was Erna Tebel. And Jenny and Gerhard and Louis Tebel. And it's taken in Krotoszyn, in Poznan, about 1913, I would guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always had it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, about 40 years later in Germany before we left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Breslau-- Jenny Tebel. That's the way she looked when we left her in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Louis Tebel, at about the same time in, in Germany when we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year about, approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: --hairstyle, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my father's mother, Elfaide Blachmann. And that must have been made in the end of, of the 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breslau. That's the one who committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The left side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The right side is the parents of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a family picture of Werner's bar mitzvah in 1936. And his parents and him and a uncle and a cousin and friends and guests of the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where this is taken, in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think in Glogau. That's where he was born, in Glogau, Schlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Werner is-- where is he in this picture? Is he on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't want me to show?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say approximately-- is he in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: The right-- there are two girls. And the next young man, that's him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents and I in Breslau in a park, at-- probably about 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you name your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Erna and Arthur Blachmann, and me, Gerda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold a minute for just a second. I'm a little bit wide. OK. There you go. OK. Stand by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father, Arthur Blachmann, at about 1937, 1938, in Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the ship, as we sat in the harbor of Havana in 1939. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably my cousin, [? Lotte ?] [? Rappaport, ?] who was in Cuba and who had sent us the visa. And they came with little boats, surrounding the, the And we were all standing on this-- on the railing, there, and shouting things down to the people in the little boats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in the picture at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, but I can't tell. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the environment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at a party on board ship of the And my parents are sitting on the right side in the back. And I am in front of my-- next to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the voyage to Cuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our cousin, [? Lotte, ?] in Cuba, who-- standing at the pier as the departs from Havana. And she's standing there, crying and waving to the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. A second. OK, and-- OK. Wide-- and then I'm just going to push in, so give me a minute, here. And [BACKGROUND NOISE] go ahead and start describing.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, it rings again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by. And start describing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's actually a picture that the took-- the news magazine-- took as we are ready to disembark in Antwerp and going off the boat. My parents are in the foreground. And I was, unfortunately, somewhere else. I didn't-- I'm not in the picture. But they are. It's taken in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. They could prob-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the yellow star that we all had to wear on an outer garment. On a coat or whatever we were wearing, we were forced to have this on, about the last year before we went to Switzerland, in Belgium. In solidarity with the Jews, a lot of Belgian people wore that, too. And all of the sudden, there were thousands of people with this yellow star, you know, see. The inscription says 'Juif,' which is French for 'Jew.'

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of Werner and me as a young couple, before the children were born and after we returned from Switzerland in 1945, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say his complete name, the complete name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Werner Marcus, yeah, and Gerda Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my two sons and me at a birthday celebration-- I think my 60th birthday, it would have been. On the right is my son, Gary Marcus, then me, Gerda Marcus Wilchfort, and then Peter Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Gary Marcus, with his wife, Miriam Marcus. And I don't know other name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my other son, Peter Marcus, and his wife, Cyd Marcus--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before they were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, again, my son, Peter, and his wife, Cyd, and their little daughter, Erica Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- these are the two grandsons, the sons of Gary and Miriam Marcus. The older one is Matthew Marcus. And the younger one is Benyamin Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in her 80s, in 80--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Erna Stern-- and she was already in her 80s-- and me, Gerda Wilchfort, at my 65th birthday, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a favorite picture of four generations-- my mother, Erna [? Stern. ?] And she is already in the nursing home. That's one of the last pictures of her. And me, Gerda, and my daughter-in-law, Cyd Marcus, and the little girl, Erica Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're done with-- is your son on his way over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda, would you introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my younger son, Peter, and his little daughter, Erica. My daughter-in-law, Cyd, had to stay home. They have a brand new baby, which is a week old today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby's name is Jennifer, Jennifer Eve, and this is Erica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else is not present, today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older son, Gary, and his wife, [? Skeeter, ?] Miriam, and their two sons, Matthew and Benyamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, would you like to say something to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah. I would just like to tell her how proud I am of her for taking the time and making the tapes. I know that it's not easy for you. But it's very important. And I'm very proud of you for doing it. And I love you very much. And it's something that we will treasure--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where's the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tape's right there, honey. [LAUGHTER] It's something we will treasure as a family, and hopefully many people will benefit from forever. And I love you. I'm going to give you a kiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I love you, too. I love little Erica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eric, do-- Erica, you want to come over here and see your daddy in the-- in the--
